associations.item1_title: AI-Extracted Eye-Tracking Features for ADHD Screening: A Tablet-Based Approach
associations.item2_title: Unraveling ADHD Through Eye Tracking: Pupil Metrics Do Not Outperform Traditional Diagnostic Methods
associations.description: Chen et al. report 89% sensitivity for AI-extracted eye-tracking features in ADHD screening, while the Unraveling ADHD study finds pupil-based metrics achieve only 57% sensitivity. The contradiction likely stems from differences in feature extraction approach (AI-integrated saccadic features vs. raw pupilometry) and analytic pipeline.
associations.significance: Resolving this contradiction is critical for determining whether eye-tracking has clinical utility for ADHD screening, and if so, which paradigm should be pursued.

research_gaps.description: No single study has directly compared AI-integrated eye-tracking features against raw pupilometric metrics for ADHD screening using the same population, apparatus, and gold standard diagnostic protocol.
research_gaps.evidence: Chen 2023 uses tablet-based ML-extracted features (n=437, positive finding) while Unraveling ADHD 2023 uses lab-based pupilometry (n=186, null finding). Different paradigms, populations, and analytical pipelines prevent direct comparison.
